raw_case:
Case report: Thoracic schwannoma as a diagnostic pitfall in both 18F-Choline PET/CT and 18F-FDG PET/CT
We report increased 18F-FDG uptake in the right posterior mediastinal region in a 70-year-old woman following the discovery of a mass in the aftermath of a bronchitis episode. We also report increased 18F-Choline uptake in the right posterior mediastinal region in a 66-year-old man with newly discovered prostate cancer, which may indicate the presence of mediastinal metastases. Both patients had a thoracic MRI showing an intense gadolinium enhancement in the same region, consistent with thoracic schwannomas, which were subsequently proven histologically. This case highlights that schwannoma is a diagnostic pitfall in both 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline PET/CT.

Introduction

A schwannoma is a generally benign soft tissue tumor that arises from Schwann cell myelin in the peripheral nervous system. It can be difficult to distinguish from malignant soft tissue tumours. The number of malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumors is relatively small, and the incidence rate is 3-10% of soft tissue sarcomas (1). Thoracic schwannomas are usually located in the posterior mediastinum (2, 3) and are often large in size (4), but rarely intrapulmonary (5, 6). On imaging, schwannomas appear in the path of a nerve, are well limited, often oval and single. MRI shows low to intermediate signal intensity on T1-weighted images, high signal intensity on T2-weighted images and intense enhancement of solid components on gadolinium-enhanced imaging. Regions of very high T2-weighted signal intensity correspond to cystic degeneration and may show only peripheral enhancement or no enhancement associated with very high signal intensity. Overall, the larger the schwannoma, the more heterogeneous it appears on all sequences (including gadolinium-enhanced images) due to cystic degeneration, hemorrhage or both (3). The metabolic characteristics of schwannomas on 18F-FDG PET-CT are not well described in the literature to differentiate benign or malignant forms. There is no association between SUV and benign or malignant schwannomas (7, 8) moreover lesions with heterogeneous FDG activity had higher SUVmax and more frequent internal non-enhancement on MRI (9). Radiolabeled choline is becoming a promising tracer in the diagnosis of glial tumors due to its low distribution rate in normal white and gray matter, leading to a high background signal-to-noise ratio compared with 18F-FDG PET/CT (10). Thus, patients with suspected recurrent glial tumors have been shown to have a more clearly defined abnormal accumulation on 18F-Choline PET/CT (11).

Case description

We first present a 70-year-old woman with no significant past medical history other than 15 pack-years of smoking. Following severe bronchitis, a right paravertebral mass measuring 62 x 49 mm was seen on chest radiography. This corresponded to a hypermetabolic lesion (SUVmax 6.7) on 18F-FDG PET, without locoregional extension. 
Figures 1A–C
.We then present the case of a 66-year-old man with a particular history of Gleason 8 prostatic adenocarcinoma. 18F-Choline PET/CT for disease extension revealed a right posterobasal lung mass almost 5 cm long with moderate uptake (SUVmax 6.4) at the posterior pleural contact. 
Figures 2A’-C’
.Complementary MRI was performed for both two lesions (panel of different MRI sequence both two cases). It showed same characteristics: heterogeneous T2 hypersignal with some microcystic areas, T1 isosignal to muscle and moderate diffusion restriction; gadolinium injection resulted in early, intense and relatively homogeneous enhancement outside cystic (or myxoid) areas. 
Figures 1D–F
 and 
Figures 2D’-F’
.Both patients (
Figure 1G
’ and 
Figure 2G
’) were operated on with complete excision in favor of a benign schwannomatous nerve lesion, with a tumor proliferation rate (Ki-67 index) of 3% for both lesions. (
Figure 1G
: In the pleura, tumor characterised by spindle cells intermingled in bundles. Absence of cytonuclear atypia. Magnification x20. 
Figure 2G’
: High magnification image (x40) of the tumor expressing the positive S100 protein in immunochemistry with strongly and diffusely signal over the entire lesion).

Discussion


18F-FDG PET/CT is used to differentiate between benign and malignant tumors (12). However, false-positive and false-negative results with 18F-FDG PET/CT are relatively common. 18F-FDG uptake in benign schwannomas varies considerably, and benign schwannomas with high FDG uptake can easily be misdiagnosed as malignant. Indeed, even with low levels of Ki-67, the lesion may be avid for 18F-FDG, as there is no correlation between Ki-67 expression and 18F-FDG uptake in schwannomas (13), and this avidity for 18F-FDG could be due to overexpression of GLUT1 or GLUT3, although this is not certain as studies give different results (14, 15).However, 18F-Choline avidity is less controversial in suspected schwannomas as it is a precursor of phosphatidylcholine, one of the main components of the cell membrane. Its specific pathophysiological pathway allows it to be used as a marker of cell proliferation in PET, helping to detect tumors that synthesize a lot of cell wall, such as prostate cancer (16). Schwannomas are composed of myelin, the precursor of which is phosphatidylcholine (17), which explains their detection on 18F-Choline PET/CT. Although it remains difficult to distinguish between a metastasis and a schwannoma in the case of 18F-Choline uptake, the unusual location of this uptake in the context of a prostate cancer metastasis could more easily point to another etiology, such as a benign tumor.

Conclusion

It seems difficult to conclude a diagnosis of schwannoma on the basis of PET-CT SUV characteristics alone, as their SUVmax seems to vary enormously. There are also several other causes of false positives, such as infectious and inflammatory processes.Although promising, 18F-Choline PET/CT cannot play a role in the management of newly diagnosed glial lesions, as it is a non-tumor-specific tracer. Biopsy remains the gold standard for accurate diagnosis.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Two patients: a 70-year-old woman and a 66-year-old man.
- **Chief Complaint:** 
  - 70-year-old woman: Incidental discovery of a mass following severe bronchitis.
  - 66-year-old man: Incidental discovery of a lung mass during evaluation for prostate cancer metastasis.
- **History of Present Illness:**
  - 70-year-old woman: Right paravertebral mass (62 x 49 mm) identified on imaging.
  - 66-year-old man: Right posterobasal lung mass (~5 cm) identified during prostate cancer workup.
- **Past Medical History:** 
  - 70-year-old woman: History of smoking (15 pack-years).
  - 66-year-old man: Gleason 8 prostate adenocarcinoma.
- **Personal History:** Not mentioned.
- **Family History:** Not mentioned.
- **Physical Examination:** None mentioned.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - PET/CT: Both lesions showed increased metabolic activity (SUVmax ~6.4–6.7) on 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline PET/CT.
  - Thoracic MRI: Both lesions demonstrated heterogeneous T2 hypersignal, T1 isosignal, and gadolinium-enhancing solid components.
  - Histology and Immunohistochemistry: Biopsy and complete excision confirmed benign schwannomas (spindle cells with positive S100 protein staining; Ki-67 index 3%).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Findings:**
   - The discovery of posterior mediastinal/lung masses with increased uptake on 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline PET/CT raised concerns for malignancy, particularly in contexts of bronchitis (infectious/inflammatory process) and a history of prostate cancer (metastasis).
2. **Considerations for Malignancy:**
   - The metabolic activity and SUVmax values were equivocal and could suggest malignancy, especially due to the Gleason 8 prostate adenocarcinoma in the 66-year-old man. However, the SUVmax correlation is unreliable in schwannomas.
3. **Role of MRI:**
   - Thoracic MRI findings of heterogeneous T2 hypersignal with gadolinium enhancement provided critical clues, as these features are consistent with benign schwannomas.
   - The absence of significant locoregional extension suggested a benign process.
4. **Histological Confirmation:**
   - Biopsy and surgical excision of both lesions confirmed the diagnosis of benign schwannomas based on spindle cell morphology, S100 positivity, and low proliferation index (Ki-67 of 3%).
5. **Ruling Out Other Diagnoses:**
   - Prostate cancer metastasis was excluded due to the unusual location of the lesion and histological findings incompatible with metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma.
   - Infectious or inflammatory processes were not supported by histology or clinical context.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis for both patients was benign thoracic schwannoma, confirmed by histological analysis. Key factors supporting the diagnosis included the MRI findings, S100 protein positivity, low Ki-67 proliferation index, and exclusion of malignant or metastatic processes.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's start by looking at the patient information. We have two elderly patients, a 70-year-old woman and a 66-year-old man. Both presented with incidental masses found during evaluations for other issues—bronchitis and prostate cancer metastasis, respectively. 

First, the woman's mass is in the right paravertebral area, which is near the spine. The man has a lung mass in the right posterobasal region. Both masses are around 5 cm, so they're sizable. Both showed increased metabolic activity on PET/CT with both FDG and Choline tracers. FDG uptake is commonly associated with malignancies because cancer cells metabolize glucose more, but Choline is often used for prostate cancer. However, the fact that both tracers showed uptake might be a clue. 

Next, the MRI findings for both lesions: heterogeneous T2 hypersignal, T1 isosignal, and gadolinium enhancement. These imaging features can be seen in various tumors, including nerve sheath tumors like schwannomas. Schwannomas are typically benign and can occur in the paravertebral area or the lung, though lung involvement is less common. 

Then, the histology and immunohistochemistry results. Both patients had biopsies and excisions confirming benign schwannomas. Spindle cells with S100 positivity are classic for schwannomas. The Ki-67 index is low at 3%, supporting a benign diagnosis since higher proliferation indices are seen in malignant tumors.

Wait, but both patients had prostate cancer? The man has Gleason 8 prostate adenocarcinoma, which is high-grade. The woman has a smoking history, which is a risk factor for lung cancer. However, the key here is that despite the initial suspicion of malignancy due to the PET/CT findings (especially since the man was being checked for metastasis), the biopsies confirmed schwannomas. 

Schwannomas can sometimes show FDG uptake on PET scans, which might lead to a false suspicion of malignancy. The use of Choline PET for prostate cancer might have picked up the schwannoma as well, since choline is involved in cell membrane synthesis and can accumulate in various tumors. 

So even though the clinical scenario for each patient might have suggested metastasis (prostate cancer in the man, possible lung cancer in the woman), the definitive diagnosis comes from the histopathology. Both masses are schwannomas, which are benign nerve sheath tumors. The presence of S100 positivity and low Ki-67 aligns with that diagnosis.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> Both patients presented with incidental masses: a paravertebral mass in the 70-year-old woman and a lung mass in the 66-year-old man. The initial clinical suspicion for malignancy was high due to the history of smoking (woman) and metastatic prostate cancer (man), supported by PET/CT findings showing increased metabolic activity with both 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline tracers.  
<step 2> Imaging characteristics (heterogeneous T2 hypersignal, T1 isosignal, and gadolinium enhancement on MRI) are consistent with nerve sheath tumors, such as schwannomas, which often display these features.  
<step 3> Definitive diagnosis hinges on histopathology and immunohistochemistry. Both cases demonstrated spindle cell morphology, strong S100 positivity (a marker for Schwann cells), and a low Ki-67 proliferation index (3%), confirming benign schwannomas.  
<step 4> The increased metabolic activity on PET/CT is atypical but not exclusive to malignancy; schwannomas can exhibit moderate FDG or choline uptake due to cellularity or inflammatory components, explaining the false-positive imaging results.  

### Answer:
Benign schwannomas.

reasoning_eval.step: Both patients presented with incidental masses: a paravertebral mass in the 70-year-old woman and a lung mass in the 66-year-old man. The initial clinical suspicion for malignancy was high due to the history of smoking (woman) and metastatic prostate cancer (man), supported by PET/CT findings showing increased metabolic activity with both 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline tracers.

Imaging characteristics (heterogeneous T2 hypersignal, T1 isosignal, and gadolinium enhancement on MRI) are consistent with nerve sheath tumors, such as schwannomas, which often display these features.

Definitive diagnosis hinges on histopathology and immunohistochemistry. Both cases demonstrated spindle cell morphology, strong S100 positivity (a marker for Schwann cells), and a low Ki-67 proliferation index (3%), confirming benign schwannomas.

The increased metabolic activity on PET/CT is atypical but not exclusive to malignancy; schwannomas can exhibit moderate FDG or choline uptake due to cellularity or inflammatory components, explaining the false-positive imaging results.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Step 1: Initial findings indicate posterior mediastinal/lung masses with increased uptake on 18F-FDG and 18F-Choline PET/CT, raising concerns for malignancy, particularly considering bronchitis and a history of prostate cancer.

Step 2: Considerations for malignancy include equivocal metabolic activity and SUVmax values, which may suggest malignancy given the Gleason 8 prostate adenocarcinoma in the 66-year-old man, though SUVmax is unreliable in schwannomas.

Step 3: Thoracic MRI findings show heterogeneous T2 hypersignal with gadolinium enhancement, consistent with benign schwannomas, and absence of significant locoregional extension supports a benign process.
Step 4: Biopsy and surgical excision confirm the diagnosis of benign schwannomas based on spindle cell morphology, S100 positivity, and a low proliferation index (Ki-67 of 3%).
Step 5: Prostate cancer metastasis is ruled out due to the unusual lesion location and histological findings incompatible with metastatic prostate adenocarcinoma.
Step 6: Infectious or inflammatory processes are not supported by histology or clinical context.

Final diagnosis: Both patients have benign thoracic schwannomas, confirmed by histological analysis, MRI findings, S100 protein positivity, and a low Ki-67 proliferation index, with malignant or metastatic processes excluded.
